author: @Simon_Rafferty
text:
Thanks Robert - in particular for your instruction on TinkerCAD.  I design with Solidworks - but frequently get asked, particularly by younger people, if there is a free CAD package they can use.  I've started referring them to your channel to show that you can use free CAD to produce good quality mechanical components - and to see the process you go through.  In many ways that's more valuable than simply how to use the product and applies to whatever CAD system you end up using.

Even without TinkerCAD, I love your experiments & projects.

author: @burnologist229
text: Can you do a video on Shape memory alloy Nitinol heat engines?

replies.author: @burnologist229
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Hmm, I couldn't quite find it, But I saw the video on Thermal lag Engines and that gave me an idea for a simple thermal lag nitinol heat engine. The engine has a single rotary valve, with the timing being dictated by the passive rate at which heat is lost to air temperature.  If a nitinol spring is put into a cylinder, and hot gas, introduced, the Nitinol Spring will expand pushing the piston upwards dropping the pressure, and lowering the temperature. A side effect of this spring's expansion is that the air is exposed to more surface area to cool. Ok let's assume that the Nitinol Spring has a transition temperature of 154F, with the air being introduced at 180F at 14.7 PSI. upon full expansion of the spring,  the pressure and temperature will drop. (14.1 PSI--154F) This doesn't take into account the passive air cooling so id imagine that the air temperature is like 130F or maybe less. Taking into account the negative pressure the Nitinol Spring which is connected to a piston is drawn back with the aid of the piston weight. Once fully retracted the rotary valve opens......

author: @FCFDave
text: In Tinkercad you can copy it from the old design, then paste in the new design and still have all your editing capabilities (ungroup/resize, etc)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes if you open two tabs and copy directly with control C, control V

replies.author: @FCFDave
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering You can also select an item, click the copy icon on the top left of the window, then open whatever project you want it in (or create a new one) and use the paste icon from the top bar again.  (Or at least could, last time I used Tinkercad) and it saves all the steps used to make it.

author: @GabrielPettier
text:
Regarding reusing parts. I had been working on tinkercad for some time and i realized that i was disconnected, and i didn't want to try to connect again and lose my work by refreshing, so i opened another tinkercad tab, opened the project again (this time connected), which was indeed the old version, went back to the first tab, selected everything and hit ctrl-c, then back to the new tab, and hit ctrl-v in it, and would you believe it, it worked! So no need to export as STL to use in a new project, ctrl-c works just as well, and you keep the all the constructions bits.

Thanks for showing me tinkercad btw, i just got started with 3D printing, and it's a lot easier than blender that i started with (for knowing the basics of it), i'll certainly experiment more with engine and other interesting mechanisms :)

author: @FuhrChris
text:
If the shafts were longer.
You could use less heat and less cold difference by using a larger Hot and cold piston.
There was a University that built a Sterling Engine in which the majority of the Hot Zone and Cold Zones were separated by an Air tight Canvas/tarp/Rubbery bellows.
Granted the Bellows portion didn't move all that fast but the gearing made fly wheel continuously move.

replies.author: @McRootbeer
replies.text: Do you remember the name of the university or the paper that talked about it? I've often wondered if a membrane displacer could be used in a sterling engine. Sounds like someone tried it.

replies.author: @FuhrChris
replies.text:
@McRootbeer How about I do you one better and video tape making a variation of that design.  They had 1 expansion point and 1 contraction point making for a single stroke Sterling Engine.  I realized that by using pie wedges you could make a 6 membrane or even an 8 Membraned unit that has 6 -8 piston pull and push locations.  
Add that to the advancement of off setting the piston so that it's main shaft thrust is fcoused out on the flywheel rim and NOT on the Axle would mean that the downward stroke would be more effective.

author: @FuhrChris
text:
I wish I had a better way to get in touch with you.

I could use your advice on a project. (That I will Vedio and share credits.)
I've gone and ordered some Molten Salt.
I'm planning on placing it in the middle of a Solar collector (I'm not expecting the Molten Salt to get so hot as to change into a Semi liquid or glow Red Hot.)
The container is Stainless Steel painted with a high temp black paint.
I'm thinking of making a protective basket for around the container.  And then when it's evening taking the whole stainless steel container inside the Airstream Trailer and just letting it radiate  out it's heat.

What I need from you is:  Am I over looking some health Hazard besides contact burns from touching the Stainless Steel container?
I\'ve considered both trying to seal the container against off gassing, and I've considered creating a hose connected to the container to take any gasses out a window.
(Eventually the Molten Salt would be stored while hot outside and I will develop a way to transfer the heat more controlled into the Airstream Trailer)

The Temperature requirements here are only a few months where it's cold by Dark.
Much of the time you only need heat in the mornings for a few hours, Thus I don't want to go with things like long running flame heat warming things like fireplace bricks.

The rest of the this project is backed up with things like the need to anchor the Solar collector  with a concrete post (It's flipped over twice.)

Thank you for your time.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I can't really advise you mate - i have no experience of molten salt as a heat  source

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
Ì think scaling is going to be a big problem for you, plus the time factor involved in input/output scheduling.
You're going to have to model the heat losses vs the area/volume problem. That seems to be the devil in the detail why previous experiments failed.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Look at Neander Motors diesel rhombic drive engines.  Austrian makers of rhombic drive engines for outboard boats and generators.  Fascinating work.  I think that they'd be more truly a semi-rhombic drive.   The drive keeps the piston perfectly aligned in the cylinder, at the expense of a taller deck size.    They once had a cruiser motorcycle that demonstrated the use.
